text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 07 September 2024 

Dr Patel  
Riverside Medical Practice  
14 Wharf Street, London SE8 3DZ  

Dear Dr Patel,  

Jordan Ellis, DOB: 22-11-1991, Hospital No: U918273 NHS No. 6401289753  
Flat 3, 21 Brook Lane, London E2 8NA 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Combined generalised and focal epilepsy (patient has both generalised and focal epilepsy)/history of mild traumatic brain injury in adolescence/anxiety symptoms related to seizure unpredictability/seasonal allergic rhinitis. 

Medication: Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily.  
Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily.  
Cetirizine 10 mg once daily when needed.  
Vitamin D3 1000 IU once daily.  

I reviewed Jordan Ellis in clinic today. They report splitting time between two households each week, staying four nights at one address and three nights at another. They keep a full set of antiepileptic medication at both locations and use a weekly pill organiser at each address. They have set identical phone reminders for morning and evening doses. No missed doses were reported over the past two months, and they bring both organisers to appointments for reconciliation. Sleep is generally adequate but varies slightly on changeover days; they deny alcohol or recreational drug use. 

Seizures: The patient describes two event types. First, focal aware episodes with twitching of the left hand and forearm lasting 20–40 seconds without loss of awareness, typically clustering in the late afternoon. Second, generalised tonic–clonic seizures occurring rarely, last reported three months ago, with post‑ictal confusion and myalgia. Home-obtained videos provided by a family member corroborate brief left distal clonic activity without secondary generalisation in several events. Importantly, ambulatory EEG performed last month demonstrated electrographic focal clonic frequent on EEG (~9/h), corresponding to the patient’s reported left upper limb jerks. There were also generalised spike–wave bursts during drowsiness without clear clinical correlate. Overall, findings support combined generalised and focal epilepsy. 

Since introducing structured reminders and duplicate medication storage at both households, the patient feels daytime focal jerks have become more predictable, often occurring during periods of stress or after poor sleep on transition days. No injuries have been reported. They do not drive and understand current DVLA guidance. 

Examination: Fully alert and oriented. Speech normal. Cranial nerves intact. Power full in all limbs; no pronator drift. No spontaneous myoclonus observed at rest. Gait normal. 

Investigations: 
- Ambulatory EEG (last month): frequent right centro‑parietal epileptiform discharges with electrographic focal clonic events recorded (~9 per hour) and intermittent generalised spike–wave during drowsiness.  
- MRI brain (last year): no acute lesion; subtle right perirolandic cortical dysplasia suspected; no interval change compared with prior imaging.  
- Bloods (two weeks ago): U&E, LFTs, FBC, vitamin D within reference ranges. 

Plan:  
- Continue Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily and Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily with the current duplication of supply at both households.  
- Reinforce use of weekly pill organisers at both locations; continue phone reminders timed identically across devices.  
- Provide seizure action plan copy at each household and ensure both cohabitants are aware of first aid and when to call emergency services.  
- Refer to psychology for brief intervention on anxiety management and transition‑day sleep hygiene.  
- No driving; patient remains aware of DVLA regulations for combined generalised and focal epilepsy.  
- Review in 6 months or earlier if generalised events recur, injuries occur, or frequency of focal clonic events increases beyond baseline. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: ambulatory EEG performed last month demonstrated electrographic focal clonic frequent on EEG (~9/h)
Electrographic focal clonic frequent on EEG (~9/h)

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes two seizure types. Generalised tonic–clonic seizures are rare, last occurred 3 months ago, so they do not determine the current highest frequency. The focal aware episodes are frequent and corroborated by ambulatory EEG, which recorded approximately 9 per hour. However, the letter does not provide a precise daily total and notes clustering in the late afternoon, so an exact per-day count cannot be reliably calculated from the information provided. Given clear evidence of frequent intra-day events but no definitive daily number, the safest normalised label is 'multiple per day.'
seizure_frequency_number: ambulatory EEG performed last month demonstrated electrographic focal clonic frequent on EEG (~9/h)
reference: Electrographic focal clonic frequent on EEG (~9/h)
